perturbation.original_text: With respect to information disclosed under this Agreement, the party to whom the information is disclosed and its Representatives shall: a. hold the information in confidence, exercising a degree of care not less than the care used by such party to protect its own proprietary or confidential information that it does not wish to disclose, and in no event less than a reasonable degree of care;
perturbation.changed_text: With respect to information disclosed under this Agreement, the party to whom the information is disclosed and its Representatives shall: a. generally hold the information in confidence, endeavoring to exercise a degree of care similar to that used by such party to protect its own proprietary or confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states 'hold the information in confidence, exercising a degree of care not less than... a reasonable degree of care'. The modified text uses 'generally hold the information in confidence, endeavoring to exercise a degree of care similar to that used by such party' which replaces a minimum standard ('not less than a reasonable degree') with a subjective one ('similar to'), weakening the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could potentially conflict with the Texas Penal Code § 31.05 regarding theft of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to maintain confidentiality, the modified clause increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure, which could lead to the misappropriation of trade secrets. The original clause establishes a higher standard of care, reducing this risk, while the modified clause makes it more ambiguous and subjective, potentially providing a loophole for individuals who misappropriate trade secrets under a weaker standard of care.

(f) Notwithstanding any other provision of this code, any police or other report of stolen vehicles by a political subdivision of this state shall include on the report any rental vehicles whose renters have been shown to such reporting agency to be in violation of Subsection (b)(2) and shall indicate that the renting agency has complied with the notice requirements demanding return as provided in this section. (g) It is a defense to prosecution under this section that: (1) the defendant secured the performance of the service by giving a post-dated check or similar sight order to the person performing the service; and (2) the person performing the service or any other person presented the check or sight order for payment before the date on the check or sight order. The following section was amended by the 89th Legislature. Pending publication of the current statutes, see H.B. 127 , 89th Legislature, Regular Session, for amendments affecting the following section. Sec. 31.05. THEFT OF TRADE SECRETS. (a) For purposes of this section: (1) "Article" means any object, material, device, or substance or any copy thereof, including a writing, recording, drawing, sample, specimen, prototype, model, photograph, microorganism, blueprint, or map. (2) "Copy" means a facsimile, replica, photograph, or other reproduction of an article or a note, drawing, or sketch made of or from an article. (3) "Representing" means describing, depicting, containing, constituting, reflecting, or recording. (4) "Trade secret" means the whole or any part of any scientific or technical information, design, process, procedure, formula, or improvement that has value and that the owner has taken measures to prevent from becoming available to persons other than those selected by the owner to have access for limited purposes. (b) A person commits an offense if, without the owner's effective consent, he knowingly: (1) steals a trade secret; (2) makes a copy of an article representing a trade secret; or (3) communicates or transmits a trade secret. (c) An offense under this section is a felony of the third degree. Acts 1973, 63rd Leg., p. 883, ch. 399, Sec. 1, eff. Jan. 1, 1974. Amended by Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 900, Sec. 1.01, eff. Sept. 1, 1994. Sec. 31.06. PRESUMPTION FOR THEFT BY CHECK OR SIMILAR SIGHT ORDER. (a) If the actor obtained property or secured performance of service by issuing or passing a check or similar sight order for the payment of money, when the issuer did not have sufficient funds in or on deposit with the bank or other drawee for the payment in full of the check or order as well as all other checks or orders then outstanding, it is prima facie evidence of the issuer's intent to deprive the owner of property under Section 31.03 (Theft) including a drawee or third-party holder in due course who negotiated the check or order or to avoid payment for service under Section 31.04 (Theft of Service) (except in the case of a postdated check or order) if: (1)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, potentially conflicting with Texas Penal Code § 31.05 regarding theft of trade secrets. The original text mandates 'a degree of care not less than... a reasonable degree of care,' establishing a clear minimum standard. The altered text uses 'generally hold the information in confidence, endeavoring to exercise a degree of care similar to that used by such party,' replacing the minimum standard with a subjective one. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'trade secret' and outlines offenses related to stealing, copying, or transmitting trade secrets without the owner's consent. By making the confidentiality obligation more ambiguous and subjective, the modified clause increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure and misappropriation of trade secrets. This introduces an ambiguity that makes the protection of trade secrets less enforceable, directly contradicting the intent of the law to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Information shall be deemed the property of the disclosing Party and, within ten (10) business days upon written request from the disclosing party, the other party will return all such information received in tangible form to the disclosing party or will destroy all such information.
perturbation.changed_text: Information may be considered the property of the disclosing Party and, following a reasonable period after a written request, the other party may return or discard such information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be deemed' to 'may be considered', 'within ten (10) business days' to 'following a reasonable period', and 'will return all such information received in tangible form to the disclosing party or will destroy all such information' to 'may return or discard such information' weakens the obligation to return or destroy the information. The new clause is significantly more ambiguous and unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change could contradict the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act (specifically, Texas Civil Practice & Remedies Code § 134.002 regarding injunctive relief). By making the return or destruction of confidential information discretionary and less timely, the modified clause weakens the protection of trade secrets. The original text offered a clear mechanism for recovering trade secrets, thus enabling a claim for injunctive relief to prevent further disclosure. The amended text creates ambiguity, making it harder to seek injunctive relief to protect such trade secrets, as the obligation of the receiving party to return the information is now weakened.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE CHAPTER 134. TEXAS THEFT LIABILITY ACT CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE TITLE 6. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS CHAPTER 134. TEXAS THEFT LIABILITY ACT Sec. 134.001. SHORT TITLE. This chapter may be cited as the Texas Theft Liability Act. Added by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 2, Sec. 4.05(a), eff. Aug. 28, 1989. Sec. 134.002. DEFINITIONS. In this chapter: (1) "Person" means an individual, partnership, corporation, association, or other group, however organized. (2) "Theft" means unlawfully appropriating property or unlawfully obtaining services as described by Section 31.03 , 31.04 , 31.06 , 31.07 , 31.11 , 31.12 , 31.13 , or 31.14 , Penal Code. Added by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 2, Sec. 4.05(a), eff. Aug. 28, 1989. Amended by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 858, Sec. 4, eff. Sept. 1, 1999. Amended by: Acts 2013, 83rd Leg., R.S., Ch. 10 (S.B. 953 ), Sec. 2, eff. September 1, 2013. Sec. 134.003. LIABILITY. (a) A person who commits theft is liable for the damages resulting from the theft. (b) A parent or other person who has the duty of control and reasonable discipline of a child is liable for theft committed by the child. Added by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 2, Sec. 4.05(a), eff. Aug. 28, 1989. Sec. 134.004. SUIT. A suit under this chapter may be brought in the county where the theft occurred or in the county where the defendant resides. Added by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 2, Sec. 4.05(a), eff. Aug. 28, 1989. Sec. 134.005. RECOVERY. (a) In a suit under this chapter, a person who has sustained damages resulting from theft may recover: (1) under Section 134.003 (a), from a person who commits theft, the amount of actual damages found by the trier of fact and, in addition to actual damages, damages awarded by the trier of fact in a sum not to exceed $1,000; or (2) from a parent or other person who has the duty of control and reasonable discipline of a child, for an action brought under Section 134.003 (b), the amount of actual damages found by the trier of fact, not to exceed $5,000. (b) Each person who prevails in a suit under this chapter shall be awarded court costs and reasonable and necessary attorney's fees. Added by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 2, Sec. 4.05(a), eff. Aug. 28, 1989.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation concerning the handling of confidential information. The original text used mandatory language ('shall be deemed', 'will return or destroy'), creating a clear obligation. The revised text uses discretionary language ('may be considered', 'may return or discard'), making the obligation ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 speaks to the Texas Theft Liability Act, it does not address the core issue of injunctive relief mentioned in the Law Explanation. However, the Law Explanation explicitly states that weakening the obligation to return or destroy confidential information hinders the ability to seek injunctive relief to protect trade secrets. Therefore, based on the Law Explanation, the changes introduce ambiguity making it harder to enforce the return of proprietary information and contradict the intent to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it will not, without the prior written consent of the other, transmit, directly or indirectly, the information received from the other hereunder or any portion thereof to any country outside of the United States.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to try not to, without notifying the other, transmit, wherever practical, the information received from the other to countries outside of the United States.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will not' with 'agrees to try not to,' 'prior written consent' with 'notifying the other,' and adding 'wherever practical' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to prevent transmitting confidential information outside the United States. This makes the restriction much less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: This weakens the legal obligation and could lead to violation of the Economic Espionage Act (18 U.S. Code § 1832), particularly if the disclosed information constitutes a trade secret. The original text strictly prohibits unauthorized transmission of information outside the US. The modification creates loopholes where transmission is permissible with mere notification or considered acceptable if deemed 'impractical' to prevent. This ambiguity could undermine the protections afforded by the EEA, as it creates circumstances where trade secrets could be illegally transferred abroad with little or no recourse.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the original contract's prohibition on transmitting information outside the United States, potentially conflicting with the Economic Espionage Act (EEA). The original text strictly forbids unauthorized transmission, but the changed text introduces ambiguity by using 'agrees to try not to,' requiring only 'notification' instead of 'prior written consent,' and adding the condition 'wherever practical.' This weakening could undermine the protections afforded by the EEA, as it creates circumstances where trade secrets could be illegally transferred abroad with little or no recourse. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 18 USC 1832, which covers theft of trade secrets; this law is potentially violated because the modified text makes it more difficult to prevent or punish the unauthorized transmission of trade secrets outside the US.
